findings.title: parties_select_by_invite using (true) exposes every party + invite_code to anon role
findings.description: Two SELECT policies on public.parties are OR'd together; the second is `using (true)`. Plus no TO clause means anon role applies. Combined with `grant select to anon` in grants.sql, anyone with the public anon key can dump all rows including invite_code.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker reads NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY from page bundle, calls REST GET /parties?select=*, dumps all invite_codes, signs in (anonymously is enabled), calls rpc('join_party', {p_invite_code}) for each leaked code, becomes a member of every household, then reads all tasks/verifications/photos through normal RLS-passing queries.
findings.impact: Full read access to every household's data. Lateral takeover of every party.
findings.recommendation: Drop policy parties_select_by_invite. Add SECURITY DEFINER function get_party_by_invite_code(text) returning (id, name) and call it from lib/db/party.ts. Rotate existing invite_codes.

findings.title: approve_verification and reject_verification trust caller-supplied approver_id
findings.description: Both SECURITY DEFINER functions take p_approver_id / p_rejecter_id as parameters and do not verify auth.uid() or party membership. grants.sql:18 grants execute to anon. Compare with create_verification at line 244 which correctly uses auth.uid().
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker obtains a verification UUID (from realtime channel, leaked log, or activity.target_id) and calls rpc('approve_verification', {p_verification_id, p_approver_id: <any-uuid>}). Function bypasses RLS, approves the verification, increments scores, attributing the action to a fabricated user.
findings.impact: Approve/reject any pending verification anywhere; falsify audit log actor.
findings.recommendation: Drop p_approver_id parameter, use auth.uid() inside the function, verify is_party_member(v_verif.party_id) before mutation. Update lib/db/verifications.ts:50-76 to drop the id arg.

findings.title: Verification photos use getPublicUrl; storage RLS commented out in repo
findings.description: Upload at line 87 uses client-supplied userId in path. line 93 calls getPublicUrl which only works if bucket is public. Storage RLS at policies.sql:197-214 is wrapped in /* */ — actual policies live only in dashboard. Even the commented version had a flaw: verifications_read_party did not actually check party membership.
findings.exploit_scenario: If bucket is public, anyone with a leaked URL has perpetual photo access. If storage RLS lacks the (storage.foldername(name))[1] = auth.uid()::text upload check, any authenticated user can upload to another user's folder. Combined with Finding 1, an attacker who walks into a household via leaked invite code lists all photo_urls and downloads them.
findings.impact: Permanent unauth photo access (depends on dashboard config).
findings.recommendation: Make bucket private; switch to createSignedUrl(path, 60*15); apply both upload-own and party-member-read storage policies for real.
findings.verification: code-traced; bucket privacy not verifiable from repo

findings.title: parties_update_members has USING but no WITH CHECK
findings.description: Update policy on parties only has USING; any column can be updated to any value by any party member.
findings.exploit_scenario: Malicious member rotates invite_code to lock out party owner from inviting anyone, renames party to harassment text, or rewrites created_by.
findings.recommendation: Add WITH CHECK that pins immutable columns. Better: move invite-rotate and rename to owner-only SECURITY DEFINER functions that check role='owner'.

findings.title: profiles_update_self lacks WITH CHECK
findings.description: Update policy on profiles has USING but no WITH CHECK.
findings.exploit_scenario: FK on id prevents impersonation, but name/emoji can be set to UTF-8 lookalikes for visual impersonation in shared party UIs.
findings.recommendation: Add `with check (id = auth.uid())`; consider charset validation on name.

findings.title: SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY present in .env.local but never imported
findings.description: grep -r 'SERVICE_ROLE' across the entire codebase returns zero matches. The key is set but unused — dead config increasing exposure surface for future careless imports and ops-time leakage.
findings.exploit_scenario: Future PR imports the key into a route without sufficient guards, bypassing all RLS. Or stack traces in Vercel logs leak it.
findings.impact: Latent risk; full DB access if leaked.
findings.recommendation: Remove from .env.local, .env.local.example, and Vercel project envs until actually needed. Rotate in Supabase dashboard regardless.

findings.title: .gstack/ not in .gitignore
findings.description: Security reports under .gstack/security-reports/ could be committed accidentally.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attack scenarios and exploit details land in public git history.

moderate_cve_advisories: GHSA-qx2v-qp2m-jg93 (postcss XSS via unescaped </style> in stringify; transitive of next; no fix available; not user-reachable)
